chunks.title: College of Engineering Computer Science and Engineering CSCE 313 Syllabus
chunks.content:
Section 500 (42151)
Introduction to Computer Systems
Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information
Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MWF
Start Time: 1:50PM
End Time: 2:40PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location: HRBB 124
Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LAB
Meeting Days: TR
Start Time: 08:25AM
End Time: 09:15AM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location: ZACH 582
Credit Hours: 4

Catalog Description
Introduction to Computer Systems. (3-2). Credit 4. Introduction to system support for application programs, both on single node and over network including OS application interface, inter-process communication, introduction to system and network programming, and simple computer security concepts; hands-on lab assignments.

Special Course Designation
None

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; grade of C or better in CSCE 312 or concurrent enrollment in CSCE 350/ECEN 350 or ECEN 350/CSCE 350.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content: By the end of the semester, students will be able to utilize basic operating-system services and C/C++ system APIs, create multi-threaded solutions, implement proper synchronization, and build applications with efficient file I/O and memory management.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Your grade in the class will be based on the following activities spread throughout the semester.

*   Labs & Programming Assignments – 50%.
*   Homeworks – 10%.
*   In-class graded assignments – 10%.
*   Three midterms – 30%. Either no final exam, or final exam in lieu of the third midterm!

Your letter grade in the class will be based on the following cutoffs where x is your cumulative score.

*   A: x ≥ 90.
*   B: 80 < x < 90.
*   C: 70 < x < 80.
*   D: 60 < x < 70.
*   F: x < 60.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Course Specific Late Work Policy
*   Late assignments will not be accepted without a reason.
*   Assignments will usually be due by 11:59 PM on the due date. To accommodate last minute submissions, we'll deduct 1% penalty per late hour on programming assignments. I.e., we'll grade the assignment as if it was submitted on time, then deduct for late time. In most cases you'll submit your assignments on Gradescope but if because of some technical hitch we ask you to mail your assignment to us, you must keep the mail in your Sent/Outbox folder, so you can later show that you mailed us the assignment on time. Please discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the TA's when possible.
*   Work submitted as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).
*   Email submissions will not be accepted in general. However, you should keep a copy (in your mail Sent/Outbox folder) of all relevant communication to us in case of disputes.
*   If you have difficulty finishing an assignment, contact the instructor/TA before the deadline.

Course Specific Attendance Policy
Attendance during lectures is not mandatory. If you are receiving federal financial assistance, the university uses your "participation” as a measure of meeting the requirements of the assistance. Physical attendance is one criterion to determine participation. You should be ok if you're turning in labs and programming assignments, taking quizzes and exams etc. However, I will take attendance as an indication of your “participation” and report it to the university when asked. I may take pictures of the classroom to verify attendance.

Makeup Work Policy
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor” (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).

"The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).

Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
§ Week 1, Jan 12. First week of class
Labs/Assignments:
*   Linux environment setup on GCP. Wait for TA instructions. Read lab0 for details.
*   GDB.
Reading:
*   Git from Scratch. We have a site license for LinkedIn learning at TAMU and you should be able to login using your Tamu email.
*   Skim through the x86-64 ISA on Prof. Eddie Kohler's Assembly Page.
*   Chapter 3 of Dive into Systems.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   Git from Scratch. We have a site license for LinkedIn learning at TAMU and you should be able to login using your Tamu email.
*   Skim through the x86-64 ISA on Prof. Eddie Kohler's Assembly Page.
*   Chapter 3 of Dive into Systems.

§ Week 2, Jan 19. MLK holiday on monday.
Labs/Assignments:
*   GDB continued
*   Time permitting: Git Overview.
*   Lab 1 on GDB.
Reading:
*   Mechanism: Limited Direct Execution—Chapter 6 of OSSTEP.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   No CSCE313_03.pdf.
*   CSCE313_02.pdf—Architectural Support for OS.
*   CSCE313_04.pdf—Introduction to Processes.

§ Week 3, Jan 26. Attendance certification due.
Reading:
*   Sections 7.1 to 7.6 in APUE.
*   A fork() in the road. Skim through the paper to get the gist of why the authors don't like the fork system call.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   Sections 7.1 to 7.6 in APUE.
*   A fork() in the road. Skim through the paper to get the gist of why the authors don't like the fork system call.

§ Week 4, Feb 2.
Labs/Assignments:
*   x86_64 ISA overview.
*   Lab exercise 2. This one is /bin/ls -l | tr a-z A-Z
*   For fun or profit, redo the assignment using posix_spawn() instead of fork().
Reading:
*   Chapter 7 of Dive into Systems.
*   x86-64 ISA. Prof. Eddie Kohler's Assembly Page
*   The x86-64 chapter of the CS107 Course Reader.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   Assembly Language on x86-64.
*   CSCE313_0101.pdf.

§ Week 5, Feb 9.
Labs/Assignments: PA #1. TBD.
Readings: Class Topics and Slides: Sections 5.1, 5.2, 5.3 in OSSTEP.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   CSCE313_06.pdf. Process API - I
*   CSCE313_07.pdf. Process API - II

§ Week 6, Feb 16. Tentative date for midterm-I Feb 18.
Labs/Assignments: Lab 3. User-space thread library.
Reading: Sections 39.1, 39.6 in OSSTEP.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   CSCE313_09.pdf. Unix File I/O.
*   CSCE313_10.pdf. UNIX I/O file descriptors, redirection and standard I/O

§ Week 7, Feb 23.
Labs/Assignments: PA #2
Reading: Sections 40.1, 40.6 in OSSTEP.
Class Topics and Slides: CSCE313_11_1.pdf. File Systems.

§ Week 8, Mar 2. Mid-semester grades are due at noon on Mar 2.
Labs/Assignments: Lab 4. Signals.
Reading: Class evaluation at the half-way mark. Signals are described in Chapter 10 of APUE, Pages 313-322. The sigaction function is described in APUE pages 349-354.
Class Topics and Slides: CSCE313_12.pdf—Signals

§ Week 9, Mar 9. Spring Break.

§ Week 10, Mar 16. Tentative date for midterm-II: Mar 18
Labs/Assignments: Lab 5.
Reading:
*   OSSTEP-Concurrency: An Introduction.
*   OSSTEP-Thread API.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   CSCE313_11_2.pdf—File systems II.
*   CSCE313_14.pdf—Concurrency and threads

§ Week 11, Mar 23.
Labs/Assignments:
*   Lab 6. Strace.
*   PA #3. Threadpools?
*   Written Homework.
Reading:
*   Condition Variables
*   Semaphores
Class Topics and Slides:
*   CSCE313_15.pdf—Synchronization
*   CSCE313_16.pdf—Semaphores

§ Week 12, Mar 30. Fri Apr 3 is Reading day.
Class Topics and Slides: CSCE313_18.pdf—Networking.

§ Week 13, Apr 6.
Reading:
*   Network IPC: Sockets. This is Sections 16.1-16.4 of APUE.
*   Beej's Guide to Network Programming—Sections 3.3, 5, 9.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   CSCE313_19.pdf—Routers, Switches, Packets, and Frames
*   CSCE313_20.pdf—Network Programming Socket API

§ Week 14, Apr 13.
Class Topics and Slides:
*   CSCE313_21.pdf—Introduction to computer security
*   CSCE313_22.pdf—Network security: the data link layer

§ Week 15, Apr 20.
Class Topics and Slides: TBD

§ Week 16, Apr 27. Tue Apr 28 last day of classes.
Class Topics and Slides: TBD

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: I'm fine if you use tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude etc. to understand the material in the class, and even to help with your homeworks. But blind copying and pasting from these tools is not permitted. You must eventually write the solution yourself, and code your labs and programming assignments yourself. Also remember that you'll have to take all exams without these tools, and a weaker understanding resulting from constant use of these tools will reflect in your exam performance.
